Rather than celebrating what is loved, the haiku documents the inability to love meaningfully. It is a masterwork of ironic restraint, where form—the haiku's brevity and imagistic tradition—becomes the vehicle for expressing emotional bankruptcy. The work succeeds precisely through its refusal to succeed emotionally.
